title: Extrarenal (Hypotonic Fluid) Water Loss
buzzwords: Hot environment with profuse sweating
Severe diarrhea or persistent vomiting
Dry mucous membranes and poor skin turgor
Low urine output with very concentrated urine
Hypotonic body fluid losses exceed sodium losses
Urine osmolality above 600 mOsm/kg with high serum sodium
Sweat sodium only 40-60 mEq/L drives free water loss
Intact ADH response producing maximally concentrated urine

title: Central Diabetes Insipidus
buzzwords: Polyuria and polydipsia with dilute urine
Onset after head trauma or neurosurgery
Pituitary mass or autoimmune hypophysitis
High serum sodium with inappropriately low urine osmolality
Posterior pituitary fails to release ADH
Urine concentrates after exogenous ADH administration
DDAVP increases urine osmolality on water deprivation test
Treat with desmopressin nasal spray

title: Nephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus
buzzwords: Polyuria unresponsive to thirst correction
Bipolar patient on long-term mood stabilizer
Hypercalcemia or chronic hypokalemia background
Kidneys fail to respond to circulating ADH
No rise in urine osmolality after DDAVP
Aquaporin-2 not inserted in collecting duct
Lithium toxicity or V2-receptor antagonist tolvaptan
Treat with thiazide or amiloride plus low-salt diet

title: Osmotic Diuresis
buzzwords: Polyuria with high serum sodium and high urine output
Uncontrolled diabetic with severe hyperglycemia
Patient receiving treatment for cerebral edema
Unexpected solute drags water into the urine
High urine osmolality but with non-electrolyte osmole
Glucosuria pulls free water and electrolytes along
Mannitol infusion produces obligate water loss
Urine electrolyte concentration lower than serum

title: Sodium Overload Hypernatremia
buzzwords: Hypernatremia without free water deficit
Iatrogenic cause during inpatient resuscitation
Hypervolemia rather than dehydration on exam
Sodium intake exceeds water intake
Large hypertonic saline bolus administration
Post-code patient given concentrated sodium bicarbonate
1000 mEq/L sodium push during cardiac arrest
True salt excess, not a water problem
